entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: Students read about the story of cartography from ancient Mesopotamia up through the discovery of Antarctica in the late 1800s and draw maps along the way. The course finishes with a term of historical geography where students follow the steps of travelers from the 19th century.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: The first two terms are the same every year and shared between both US and Canadian courses. Term 3 changes for each historical time period.

rows.label: Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.)
rows.books: Mapping the World with Art||United States Atlas 7th Ed.||Student World Atlas 6th Ed.||This Strange Wilderness: The Life and Art of John James Audubon

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: This Strange Wilderness: The Life and Art of John James Audubon

blocks.text: The steps below are generally true for most geography lessons. Sometimes several lessons might build together or focus on a particular area (like Map Work).
Through questions, observations, an activity, or recalling the last lesson, introduce the topic for the day.
Answer questions on the map or on making your own maps.
Read the day's passage out loud or have the student read independently.
Have students retell the passage either orally or in writing. They may want to use maps or pictures to tell back.
The lesson may contain more map work as students make their own maps or add to ongoing maps.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: Ideally, you should have a notebook that is at least 8 x 10 inches, so you have room to draw your own maps and also glue in maps that are printed off.

➜ INTRO
This year, we are learning about mapmaking and global exploration. You will be learning to draw your own maps as well. You may want to first practice outlining the map on a whiteboard or another piece of paper before putting it in your notebook. As time permits on drawing days, you are encouraged to use watercolors or colored pencils to fill in your outlines (see the link for examples done by students). Your Geography notebook will be used on both days of the week. Make sure you label each entry with the date or lesson number. There will be days when you will be prompted to follow the instructions for an activity in the Activity Section of your Mapping the World with Art book.
∞ Image Link: Watercolor Map Examples

➜ READ
Reading 1: The Very Earliest Maps

➜ NARRATE & NOTEBOOK
1\. Narrate orally what you remember about the reading.
2\. Then, in your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can look back at the text as needed. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw a concept map or pictures with captions. You can be as creative as you like. We provided examples of other students' work. Typically, each two-page spread in your notebook will have a narration on one side and your map drawings on the other. There may be weeks in which you will have two drawings per two-page spread (especially in Term 3).
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 1
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 2
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 3

➜ DRAW
p.1-2 Map Drawing 1 Mesopotamia (use the book or video option)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. For example, under the map of Mesopotamia, you could say, "The earliest maps were drawn in clay by the people of Mesopotamia."

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Mesopotamia as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Mesopotamia

2\. In your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw diagrams or pictures with captions.

p.3-4 Map Drawing 2 Nile River (Use the book or video option)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. This map pertains to the first reading. You might have to look back to remember what was said about Egypt and the earliest maps.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Nile River as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of the Nile River
lessons.teacherNotes:

Check out this video for a deeper look at the history of cartography.
∞ Video Link: Origin of the World Map

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, Studendt World Atlas, notebook

NOTE: You will finish your map of Greece this week. If you get done earlier, you can practice drawing the map on a whiteboard or another piece of paper.

➜ DRAW
p.5-7 Map Drawing 3 Greece (Finish your map of Greece)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Greece as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Greece

➜ MAP QUESTION
1.Looking at the a modern Map of Greece in your Student World Atlas p.82-83, what countries border Greece? (Turkey, Bulgaria, N. Macedonia, and Albania)
2.What is the capital city of Greece? (Athens)
3.What is the biggest island of Greece? (Crete)
4.Name the bodies of water near Greece? (Ionian Sea, Aegean Sea and Mediterranean Sea)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: There is no reading for today. So you will first review a previous map drawing.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ WARM UP
On a whiteboard or piece of paper, draw a map of the Holy Land (5A)

➜ DRAW
p.11-12 Map Drawing 5B The Arabian Peninsula
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Arabian Peninsula as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of the Arabian Peninsula
∞ Image Link: Rub-al-Khali Desert in Saudi Arabia

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What bodies of water surround the Arabian Peninsula? (Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, and Gulf of Aden)
2.What countries are part of the Arabian Peninsula? (Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Oman, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait)
3.What city lies at one of the highest elevation points in the Arabian Peninsula? (Sanaa) it is a city in Yemen, near the Sarawat Mountains, it sits at an elevation of approximately 2,300 meters (7,500 feet) above sea level.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: There is no reading today, only map drawing.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ WARM UP
Draw the Italian Peninsula on a whiteboard or piece of paper (Map 4)

➜ DRAW
p.18 Map Drawing 8B Adding France to the Iberian Peninsula

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Iberian Peninsula as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of the Iberian Peninsula

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What are the two main countries that occupy the Iberian Peninsula? (Spain and Portugal)
2.Which microstate is between Spain and France? (Andorra) It is found on the eastern part the Pyrenees Mountain range.
3.What British Overseas Territory is located at the southern tip of the peninsula? (Gibraltar)
4.What is the largest city and capital of Spain? (Madrid)
5.What is the capital of Portugal? (Lisbon)

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ WARM UP
Draw a map of Britain (12A) on a whiteboard or a piece of paper. Label the items in books.

p.28-31 Finish drawing Africa (Map 13).
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Africa as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Africa

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What borders Africa to the west? (Atlantic Ocean)
2.Where do the waters of the Meditarranean Sea and Red Sea meet? (The Suez Canal)
3.What is the highest mountain peak in Africa found not far from Lake Victoria? (Kilimanjaro) Located in Tanzania, reaches 5,895 meters/19,340 feet above sea level.

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

p.32-33 Map Drawing 14 Central America and the Caribbean (finish)

It will be helpful to look at this map of Central America and the Caribbean as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Central America and the Caribbean

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What countries make up Central America? (Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama)
2.What type of landform is the narrowest part of Central America? (Isthmus-specifically, Isthmus of Panama)
3.Which Caribbean island is the largest? (Cuba)
4.Name the islands located in the North Atlantic Ocean but not in the Caribbean Sea. (The Bahamas, Turks and Caicos Islands, and Bermuda)

links.text: Map of Central America and the Caribbean

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ DRAW
p.34-36 Map Drawing 15 South America (finish)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of South America as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of South America

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What is the largest country in South America? (Brazil) the smallest? (Suriname) Note: French Guiana is smaller than Suriname but it is politcally a region of France.
2.Which lines of latitude does Brazil fall between? (5°N to 34°’S approximately)
3.What countries form the western coast of South America? (Colombia, Ecuador, Peru and Chile). 
4.What ocean borders the western coast? (Pacific Ocean) The eastern coast? (Atlantic Ocean)

➜ REVIEW
Spend your time reviewing the different maps by drawing them on a whiteboard or piece of paper. Start with the ones you remember the least. If you did not have an opportunity to use your watercolors or color pencils, this would be a good time to go back and add color to the maps in your notebook.

➜ VIEW	
∞ Image Link: Map Examples

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: Your map of the Malay Peninsula and southern Asia will take 2 lessons. So set your timer, and after 30 minutes, you can stop and finish up the map next time.

➜ READ
Reading 16: Zheng He

➜ NARRATE & NOTEBOOK
1\. Narrate orally what you remember about the reading.
2\. In your notebook, include some narration. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw diagrams or pictures with captions.

➜ DRAW
You will begin the map today and finish it in the next lesson.
p.37-40 Map Drawing 16 The Malay Peninsula and southern Asia (in the map section, it is titled "Southeast Asia Basics.")

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Southeast Asia as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Southeast Asia

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: You will finish your map of the Malay Peninsula and southern Asia this week. If you get done earlier, you can practice drawing this map or another on a whiteboard or another piece of paper.

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time?

➜ DRAW
p.37-40 Map Drawing 16 the Malay Peninsula and southern Asia (finish) 
(In the map section, it is titled "Southeast Asia Basics.")
Be sure to include a sentence describing how the map pertains to the reading.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Southeast Asia as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Southeast Asia

➜ MAP QUESTION (Map Link)
1.What archipelago of large islands is located north of the Tropic of Cancer? (Japan) What body of water separates these islands from the mainland? (the Sea of Japan and the East China Sea)
2.What island is located off the southern tip of India? (Sri Lanka)
3.What country occupies the northern part of Asia? (Russia)
4.What is the largest country in Asia? (China)
5.Between what lines of latitude and longitude does Mongolia lie? (41° and 52°N latitude and 87°and 119° E longitude)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 49
lessons.body:
John Audubon: Beloved Boy
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, World Atlas (optional), notebook, and map links  

➜PREP
In your notebook, you should set aside the last 5 pages for this term's day 1 assignment. Label the section "Audubon’s Wilderness Travels." You will also need a blank 1850’s United States map to mark the places mentioned in your readings. 
∞Map Link: 1850s U.S. Map

➜INTRODUCTION
1. In this term, we will have follow the life of the naturalist John James Audubon as he explores the wilderness of America during the late 1800s, a time when the new frontier still had much to learn about the land’s wildlife.  
2. You may want to review vocabulary as needed. 

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Materials: United States Atlas p.27, World Atlas or world map link
∞Map Link: World Map
1\. On the world map link or in a Student World Atlas (p.30-31) , can you locate Haiti and France? The island of Haiti has two main languages: Haitian Creole and French. Due to its history of French colonization, the island still speaks and uses French in formal settings. The majority of people, however, speak Haitian Creole (French-influenced). 
2\. Now look at p.27 in your United States Atlas. Locate Pennsylvania. All these places were significant to John James Audubon. He was born in Haiti, raised in France, and then came to Pennsylvania when he was a young man.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
This Strange Wilderness Prologue-Ch.1 p.xv-7 “In the heat” - ”a great wilderness.”

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook, 1850s U.S. map
Print the 1850s map. Cut along the border of the page. Tape or paste this page on your first blank page in the “Audubon’s Wilderness Travels” section.  You will be tracking the places he visits.  Label this map, "Audubon's Travels".
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
observation: careful watching to collect information

naturalist: someone who studies plants and animals in nature

medium: the material used by an artist (e.g., watercolor)

field notes: short written records made during observation

What did you read last time?

This Strange Wilderness Ch.2 p.9-16 “Audubon sat in” - ”on the frontier.”

➜ VIEW
This is an engraving of a flatboat from the 1800s similar to what Audubon would gave traveled down the Ohio River in. 
∞ Image Link: Flatboat of 1800s

Look at either the painting of the Eastern Phoebe on p. 8 or the Wild Turkey on p. 14. Similar to picture study, describe what you see—colors, textures, background, foreground, etc.

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the state of Pennsylvania on your map. Write Pennsylvania inside the borders. Use a light shade of colored pencils to shade in the state.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
émigré / immigrant: a person who moves from one country to another

plantation: a large farm where crops and, previously, enslaved labor was used

apprentice: a learner who trains under an expert

• EXTRA HELPING
You may want to watch part of this video to learn more about the painting of Wild Turkey.
∞ Video Link: Audubon's Wild Turkey (ends at 4:12)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 66
lessons.body:
Final Project: Create a World Map
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, materials will vary depending on which option you choose. Check your book p.136, for the various possibilities.

PREP: Parents, this should be a final project that requires at least 4 lessons to complete (including this lesson). Make sure to read through the instructions before beginning. Once you and your student have decided which option they will work on, begin to gather the materials needed. Have a designated space where they can put their final project away safely to be worked on the next time. Maps are not perfect; have the student plan out their project in quadrants to ease the process. Ideally, they should be working from memory as best they can. The book recommends that you help them include a few reference points before they begin, such as longitude and/or latitude lines like the Equator, Prime Meridian, or Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, to name a few. (Light lines for these points; they are mere references for their map drawings.)

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
p.135 Activity Section: Final Project: Creating a World Map
Create a World Map following the instructions in your book, starting on p.135. Take four weeks to complete, working one quadrant each week.
Have fun!

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 67
lessons.body:
John Audubon: The Strange Wilderness, Then and Now
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook 

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?    

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.15, 115
1\. Locate Texas on the map. What states border Texas? (Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma, New Mexico) 
2\. Locate Missouri on the map. What states border Missouri? (Arkansas, Oklahoma, Kansas, Nebraska, Iowa, Illinois, Kentucky, and Tennessee)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.8-9 p.80-90 “All we cared” - ”nature around me.”

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the states of Texas and Missouri on your map. Write their names inside the borders. Use a different light shade of colored pencils to shade in each state.  How colorful is your map? Are you impressed with all the places Audubon traveled to? 

➜ WRITE
On the last page in your Audubon section, write a letter to Audubon reflecting on how you feel about his contribution to ornithology and geography.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
legacy: something left behind for future generations

conservationist: a person working to protect nature

ethics: principles about right and wrong (introduce briefly: collecting vs. modern non‑lethal study)

citizen science: community volunteers contributing to scientific data (e.g., bird counts)

lessons.title: 30m Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) - Lesson 72
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Mapping the World with Art

terms.content:
· Mapping the World with Art: Tell about Marco Polo and why he was important to the history of maps. OR Tell about Henry the Navigator and his contributions to map-making.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Italy, Greece, the Iberian Peninsula, Britain, Israel, India, Africa, and South America.

· Mapping the World with Art: Tell about the unsuccessful attempts by the Europeans to find a Northeast Passage to the Orient. OR Tell about the man from the Dutch East India Company and his experience in Japan.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Scandinavia, the Philippines, the Low Countries of Europe, and Japan.

· This Strange Wilderness: Describe how Audubon's passion for birds was influential in exploring the American Wilderness. Be sure to use a specific example of a location he experienced.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Australia, New Zealand, Alaska, North America, and Antarctica.
